title: Lesson 10: 🛠️ Build a Real Page — HTML Checkpoint

content.explanation:
🚦 You've Reached a Checkpoint

This lesson is different from the last nine. There's no new tag to learn here — instead, this is where you PROVE you can combine everything from Lessons 1 to 9 into one real, working page. This matters more than any single lesson before it, because real development isn't about knowing individual tags in isolation — it's about combining them to build something complete.

🧱 Everything You've Learned So Far

  • Lesson 1–2: What HTML is, tags, elements, attributes
  • Lesson 3: Headings (h1-h6) and paragraphs
  • Lesson 4: Links and images
  • Lesson 5: Ordered and unordered lists
  • Lesson 6: div and span for grouping content
  • Lesson 7: Forms and inputs
  • Lesson 8: Tables for tabular data
  • Lesson 9: Semantic HTML5 (header, nav, main, section, article, footer)

This checkpoint asks you to use a meaningful combination of these in a single page — a simple personal profile page, the kind of thing that's often the very first project every real developer builds.

🔒 Why This Lesson Gates Your Progress

CSS (starting next lesson) is about STYLING content. If you move on without being able to comfortably structure a real page, you'll spend the entire CSS module fighting with broken or messy HTML instead of focusing on what CSS actually teaches you. This checkpoint exists to protect your progress, not slow it down — getting this solid now saves you frustration later.
content.syntax_breakdown:
🧩 Your Task Breakdown

You're building a simple personal profile page. Here's a suggested structure to think through before you start typing:

<header>
  <h1>Your Name</h1>
</header>

<main>
  <section>
    <h2>About Me</h2>
    <p>A short paragraph about yourself</p>
  </section>

  <section>
    <h2>My Skills</h2>
    <ul>
      <li>Skill one</li>
      <li>Skill two</li>
    </ul>
  </section>
</main>

<footer>
  <p>Contact info or a link</p>
</footer>

Notice this single structure pulls together semantic tags (Lesson 9), headings (Lesson 3), paragraphs (Lesson 3), and lists (Lesson 5) all at once — exactly how real pages are actually built, not as isolated examples.

💡 Approach It Step by Step

Don't try to write the whole thing in one go. Build it the way real developers do:

  • 1. Lay down the semantic skeleton first (header, main, footer) — empty
  • 2. Fill in your header with a heading
  • 3. Fill in main with your sections, one at a time
  • 4. Add your footer content last

This step-by-step habit will serve you for your entire coding journey — build structure first, fill in details after.
content.common_mistakes:
⚠️ Common Checkpoint Mistakes

  • Trying to add CSS styling here — resist the urge. This checkpoint is purely about HTML structure. Styling is next module.
  • Skipping semantic tags and just using div — the whole point of this checkpoint is proving you can combine what you learned, including Lesson 9.
  • Rushing without testing — run your code as you build it, not just at the very end, so you catch unclosed tags early.

editor_sandbox.starter_code:
<!-- 🛠️ HTML Checkpoint: Build your profile page below -->
<!-- Use what you learned in Lessons 1-9 -->

mini_challenge.instruction:
🛠️ CHECKPOINT TASK:

Build a personal profile page using AT LEAST:
1. A <header> with your name in an h1
2. A <main> containing at least 2 <section> elements (e.g. About Me, My Skills)
3. At least one <ul> or <ol> list inside one of your sections
4. A <footer> with a closing paragraph
5. Somewhere on the page, include this exact phrase in a paragraph: html checkpoint complete

This proves you can combine structure, headings, lists, and semantic tags into one real page.
mini_challenge.hint:
👉 Minimum working structure:

Your Name

About Me

html checkpoint complete

My Skills

  • HTML
  • Problem Solving

Thanks for visiting

quiz_engine.question: Why does this checkpoint matter before moving on to CSS?
options: It doesn't really matter, it's just a formality
Messy or weak HTML structure makes the upcoming CSS module much harder to learn properly
CSS doesn't actually need any HTML to work
quiz_engine.explanation_feedback: Correct 🎉 CSS styles existing HTML — if your structure is shaky, you'll spend CSS lessons fighting broken markup instead of learning styling. Solid HTML now makes everything after this easier.

takeaways: You combined headings, paragraphs, lists, and semantic tags into one real page
Real development is about combining fundamentals, not just knowing tags in isolation
Building structure first, then filling in content, is a habit that scales to every project
You're now ready to learn CSS and make this exact page look great

cheat_sheet.Checkpoint Goal: Combine Lessons 1-9 into one real working page
cheat_sheet.Approach: Build the skeleton first, then fill in content section by section
cheat_sheet.Next Up: CSS — making this page actually look good
